evidence_quotes.quote: 'gacha payments' which offer no guaranteed outcome, hence any form of lootbox-adjacent methodologies.

Addiction is a core issue in this case and warrants a restriction on usage of such methods which can cause a existence-threatening situation for those individuals even in products directly catered to adult individuals.

The removal of goods (from indefinite licenses) of customers without their agreement is a prevalent practice nowadays, one which is not upholding common market practices established since economy started to even exist.

A product was promised but not delivered when promised or even as promised.

paragraphs.text: The DFA is already fairly well designed, albeit a few more suggestions of inclusions which have problems nowadays: The creation of 'reward based crowdfunding' is currently not well defined in law. While adjacent to already existing customer protection laws for sales of goods the situations for indefinite licenses as endlessly replicable goods theoretically need better inclusion and laws related to those, both to protect people buying into sales promises to actually get the product they're backign as well as creators to allow the respective lenience needed which relates to mandatory design changes caused by problems which couldn't have been foreseen well, especially in terms of auxiliary licensing and programming hurdles. The second major aspect is conservation. The practice of the removal of goods (from indefinite licenses) of customers without their agreement is a prevalent practice nowadays, one which is not upholding common market practices established since economy started to even exist. Also the inability to preserve those products for the sake of developers exploiting the situation to increase their profits is in no manner customer friendly. Production of new products is supposed to happen based on demand and innovation, not on destruction of formerly existing products to then provide similar ones under a new name. This is a - plainly spoken - apalling but sadly normalized practice nowadays and should've a place in modern regulatory measures. Further measures should not only be focused on young people but also personalities which have the general ability to interact in a sensible manner with their environment but a proclivity to act in a easily exploitable way. Those include for example impulsive personality types, be it inherent or as a side effect of example ADHD. Addiction is a core issue in this case and warrants a restriction on usage of such methods which can cause a existence-threatening situation for those individuals even in products directly catered to adult individuals. Especially in video-games those are the practives of 'gacha payments' which offer no guaranteed outcome, hence any form of lootbox-adjacent methodologies. One often discussed exception many people argue is the Trading Card Game example. This has no underlying issues in a digital space though. The usage of chance-based acquisition has no meaning for deck-building in a digital environment as the chance-based packs are invented because of the existence of drafts. Those can be monetized directly with a flat rate for the usage of this option while generalized playing and acquisition of cards on a chance-based basis has no functionality in this environment. Examples of modern issues with video games as a short overview: 'The Crew' - Removal of a indefinite license from users through the active destruction of the product with de-listing. Access is lost and hence the value of the goods acquired. 'Last Epoch' - Release after a crowdfunding without adhering to the core pillars promised inside of it as well as not adhering to the development timeline. Promised release 2020 but instead happened 2024, protection for customers needs to happen in such cases. Further there is a complete lack of promised mechanics as well as the complete PvP mode simply not existing. This is not adhering to the reasoning why people have paid into the crowdfunding and should hence enforce a mandated option for a refund at the time of release or upon passing the deadline in a substantial manner. A product was promised but not delivered when promised or even as promised.
